title: What Does the Event Still Want Me to Know
subtitle: A journal prompt set for listening to the event that will not let you go
excerpt: A twenty-minute journal prompt set for the event that will not let you go. The event has been signalling for a reason. The prompts give it a small marked-out window to be heard, and a close at the end so the listening does not run all day.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The unsent-letter and the writing-to-the-event shapes are staples of writing on trauma, grief, and forgiveness.

practice.whenToUse: Use in a calm half-hour when you want to listen to what the event is still asking. Sit with the prompts on your own. Have one trusted person you could talk to after if it gets heavy.
practice.whenNotToUse: Not in a high-anxiety moment. Not in the week of an acute trauma. The practice is for the slow work, not the live moment.

content.text: A twenty-minute journal prompt set for the event that will not let you go. The prompts give it a small marked-out window to be heard. The close at the end is part of the practice.
New to journal prompts as a practice? Read
journal prompts as practice
first.
The practice
Warm-up
What is the event, in one short paragraph?
Write the short version. Not the long story. The minimal facts. Three or four sentences.
What does my body do when the event comes up?
Where does it go in the body. Chest, throat, belly, shoulders. Name it. Two or three sentences.
Closer in
What has the event been asking me to know?
Listen for the signal underneath the catching. There is usually one. "I needed someone to see what was happening." "I am not safe in that room." "I have been alone with this for a long time."
What have I been afraid would happen if I really heard it?
There is usually a fear that keeps the listening at arm's length. Name it. The fear is not a sign you should not listen. It is a sign of how much the listening matters.
What is one small step I can take this month, in the world?
A talk with one trusted person. A first appointment with a counsellor. A boundary with a family member. A piece of writing for nobody but you. One small step, in the world, not just in your head.
Closing
Close the journal. Stand up. Drink a glass of water. Step outside for a minute if you can. The listening was for this twenty-minute window. The rest of the day is for the rest of your life.
Where this practice comes from
Original to homemade.education. The writing-to-the-event shape is a common one. It appears in books on trauma, on grief, and on forgiveness.
